text: This quarterly progress report of the Molten-Salt Reactor Program records the technical progress of the research at the Laboratory, under its Contract W-7405-eng-26, on power-producing reactors fueled with circulating fused salts.
The report is divided into two major parts: 1.
Reactor Design Study and 2.
Until July 1, 1957, the Molten-Salt Reactor Program was largely a design study, with only token expenditures for experimental work.
As of July 1, the program was expanded to include experimental work on materials.
A further augmentation of the program occurred on October 1, 1957, when personnel and facilities for additional research and experimentation became available.
Critical-inventory calculations revealed that for a 9-ft-dia core, the minimum inventory would be about $100\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ .
The volume of fuel in the external system was taken to be $340\mathrm{ft}^3$ for a power level of $600\mathrm{Mw}$ (thermal).
Regeneration ratios were obtained as a function of inventory for a 600-Mw system, with thorium concentration as a parameter.

From this envelope it may be concluded that (1) with a fuel inventory of a trifle over $100\mathrm{kg}$ , a regeneration ratio of 0.4 can be obtained (in an 8-ft core), (2) by doubling the inventory a regeneration ratio of 0.6 can be obtained (also in an 8-ft core), (3) by doubling it again a regeneration ratio of 0.65 can be obtained (in a 10- to 11-ft core), and (4) further investment of fuel would have a negligible effect on the regeneration ratio.

A design-point selection is to be attempted by balancing fuel savings by regeneration against inventory and processing charges; however, it appears at present that $400\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ may be an economical maximum for these systems.
A few calculations on systems fueled with $\mathsf{U}^{233}$ have indicated much lower critical inventories and better regeneration ratios than those obtained for $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ fuel.

It was estimated that for operation of the Reference Design Reactor at 600 Mw in a core vessel 6 ft in diameter with 1 mole $\%$ ThF $_4$ in the fuel, core gamma rays will liberate $13.4 \, \text{W/cm}^3$ in the core vessel wall.
Heating by gamma rays emitted in the blanket was found to be $0.97 \, \text{W/cm}^3$ , and capture gamma rays originating in the wall were found to contribute $1.63 \, \text{W/cm}^3$ to the heating.
The calculations were made for a pure nickel core vessel, and the results are somewhat lower than those to be expected from the calculations now being made for an INOR-8 alloy vessel.
The electrical output from a 600-Mw (thermal) reactor would be 258.6 Mw with the sodium system, and 295.8 Mw with the mercury system.

A coordinated program is under way for the investigation of container materials for molten salts that will permit operation of a molten-salt reactor for long periods of time at temperatures up to $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Inconel is the best suited of the commercially available materials; but, since its corrosion resistance and high-temperature strength are marginal, the alloy INOR-8 has been developed.

Mechanical property studies of welded joints of a nickel-molybdenum alloy similar to INOR-8 have indicated that the alloy is weldable and that the mechanical properties of the joint are satisfactory both in the as-welded and the aged conditions.
Efforts were made to resolve discrepancies in reported melting points of $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ , and additional confirmation of the reported value of $545^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ resulted.

Preliminary experiments with NaF-BeF $_2$ (57-43 mole %) have shown the solubility of CeF $_3$ in this mixture to be considerably less than in NaF-ZrF $_4$ mixtures and to be more temperature sensitive.
It may be seen that the curve for $1 \, \text{mole} \, \% \, \text{ThF}_4$ does not conform to the general pattern.

It is thought that increasing the thorium concentration in the 10-ft core decreases the fission cross section more or less uniformly so that the critical concentration rises regularly.
In the 6-ft cores the spectrum is already hard.
The spectrum is hardened in the following three ways: (1) by decreasing core size, (2) by increasing thorium concentration, and (3) by increasing uranium concentration.
If the spectrum is hardened relatively less in the 6-ft core by the addition of thorium, the decrease in uranium cross section is relatively less; hence, less uranium is needed, and the spectrum

The volume of fuel in the external system (pump, pipes, heat exchangers) was taken to be $340\text{ft}^3$ , which corresponds to a power level of $600\text{Mw}$ (thermal).
It may be seen that a minimum inventory of about $100\text{kg}$ of U<sup>235</sup> is obtained at a core diameter of 9 ft.
The corresponding power density in the core is $55\text{W/cm}^3$ (average) and the specific power is $6\text{Mw}$ per kilogram of fuel in the system.

The principal conclusions to be drawn are that (1) with a fuel inventory of a trifle over $100\mathrm{kg}$ , a regeneration ratio of 0.4 can be obtained (in an 8-ft core), (2) by doubling the
Regeneration Ratios in Two-Region, 600-Mw, Molten-Salt Reactors.
Optimum Regeneration Ratios for Two-Region, 600-Mw, Molten-Salt Reactors.

inventory a regeneration ratio of 0.6 can be obtained (also in an 8-ft core), (3) by doubling it again a regeneration ratio of 0.65 can be obtained (in a 10- to 11-ft core), and (4) further investment of fuel would have a negligible effect on the regeneration ratio.
The optimum diameter appears to increase steadily after the inventory increases beyond $200\mathrm{kg}$ .
A plot of $\eta - 1$ is also included in Fig.
1.6 to give the upper limit on the regeneration ratio.
The interval between the envelope and the $\eta - 1$ curve represents parasitic absorptions in fluorine, etc.

1.6, however, it would seem that, although $\eta - 1$ approaches a minimum at an inventory of $600\mathrm{kg}$ , further significant reduction in parasitic absorptions can be obtained only by enormous increases in the inventory.
This will be attempted; however, it does not seem likely that it would pay to invest more than $400\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ in such systems.

It must be remembered that the results discussed above apply only to the initial, clean state; that is, they apply only in the absence of fission products and uranium isotopes (other than $\mathsf{U}^{238}$ ).
In the first place, the $\mathsf{U}^{233}$ generated in the blanket will have a fission cross section in these epithermal reactors about twice as high as that of $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ .
A few calculations on systems fueled with $U^{233}$ have been completed.
Four cases, in which core diameters range from 5 to 10 ft and the $ThF_4$ concentration is 0.25 mole %, are described in Table 1.2.
The results are compared with those for corresponding $U^{235}$ systems in Fig.
The critical inventories are much lower with $U^{233}$ fuel, and the regeneration ratio is much better.
It is hoped that regeneration ratios well above 0.9 can be achieved at inventories below 500 kg.
With the use of the new data, it was estimated that during operation at 600 Mw in a core vessel 6 ft in diameter with 1
mole $\%$ ThF $_4$ in the core (see Case 63 in Table 1.2 of preceding section of this report), core gamma rays will liberate $13.4~\mathrm{W/cm}^3$ in the core vessel wall.
Similarly, the heating effected by capture gamma rays emitted in the blanket was estimated to be 0.97 w/cm³ at the inside surface of the core vessel.
The previously obtained value of 0.032 w/cm³ (for an INOR-8 core vessel) was obviously in error by an order of magnitude and will be recomputed.
The gamma heating effected by capture gamma rays originating in the nickel wall was estimated to be $1.63\mathrm{w/cm}^3$ .
In an INOR-8 alloy vessel, this contribution will be approximately twice as great because of captures in the molybdenum of the alloy.
Calculations for an INOR-8 core vessel are being made.
A study was made of two thermodynamic systems for producing power from a molten-fluoride-salt reactor capable of producing 600 Mw of heat.

One system involves transferring heat from the fuel salt to a coolant salt to sodium to water and achieves turbine steam conditions of 1800 psia at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , with reheat to $980^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Mercury vapor at 180 psia and $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ would expand through a turbine and exhaust at 7 psia and $605^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The condensing mercury would generate steam at 1250 psia, which would be superheated in two steps to $950^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ by mercury vapor bled from the turbine and taken directly from the line.

A pressure of 180 psia will provide saturated mercury vapor at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , while a 16-ft additional head (required to give a 270-psia total pressure) will result in a saturation temperature of $1075^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Downward flow of the mercury in the short leg of the J, counter to the salt flow, will raise the temperature of the nonboiling mercury to $1075^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
To avoid difficulties caused by vaporizing too large a fraction of the mercury, a recirculation ratio of 10 to 1 will be employed.
In the binary mercury-steam cycle, the mercury turbine will produce $35\%$ of the power and the steam turbine will produce $65\%$ .
The combined turbine heat rate will be 6940 Btu/kwhr, which gives $49.3\%$ efficiency.
The sodium system would have a turbine heat rate of 7920 Btu/kwhr, or $43.1\%$ turbine efficiency.
The heat transfer area from fuel to water for the sodium system is $270\mathrm{ft}^2/\mathrm{Mw}$ (electrical), that is, about one-third or one-fourth the area in a conventional fossil-fuel furnace.
A comparison of the characteristics of the sodium and mercury systems is presented in Table 1.4

The metallurgical work of the Molten-Salt Reactor Program has as an objective the provision of a container material for the molten salts that will have sufficient structural strength and compatibility with the molten salts to permit operation of a reactor for long periods of time at temperatures up to $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
1. corrosion resistance to the fused fluorides of interest for long times (5 to 20 years) at temperatures up to $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ,
In an effort to obtain an alloy with better corrosion resistance and strength in fused-salt environments, the alloy INOR-8 has been developed.

Tests at higher-than-MSR service conditions have indicated the corrosion resistance of INOR-8, in the fuel mixtures $\mathrm{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4}$ (50-46-4 mole %) and NaF-KF-LiF-UF $^4$ 11.2-41-45.3-2.5 mole %), to be ten times better than that of Inconel.
Therefore the properties of INOR-8 are being investigated at MSR service conditions.
Further studies of Inconel will be made in order to provide a basis for comparison with the new data obtained for INOR-8.
The metallurgical program for investigating INOR-8 and Inconel may be divided into three major categories: material property studies, fabrication investigations, and supporting work.

Tensile tests will be conducted at many temperatures in the range from room temperature to $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and creep tests will be run in air and in fused salts to estimate the limiting strain for long-term operation.
A program for obtaining the information required to make INOR-8 a commercial item has been developed.
At the present time there are two sources of INOR-8.

It has been purchased from Haynes Stellarite Company, on a purchase order, at a cost of about $5.00 per pound of delivered alloy, and it has been produced on a subcontract basis by the Westinghouse Electric Corporation.
Orders on the Haynes Stellarite Company are acceptable provided that 10,000-lb heats are purchased.
contract will have to be made in order to supply the material requirements for INOR-8 at ORNL.
Items currently scheduled for delivery are 200 ft of $\frac{1}{8}$ -in. sched-40 pipe, 100 ft of 3-in. sched-40 pipe, and a small quantity of welding wire.

of test, termed the circular-groove test, utilizes a $3/32$ -in.-wide, 2-in.-dia, $3/8$ -in.-deep, circular groove machined in a $4 \times 4$ in. specimen of $1/2$ -in. plate.
Test samples of heats SP-16 and 30-38, together with samples of four other alloys for comparison, are shown in Fig.
It may be seen that the restraint of the specimen caused complete circumferential cracks in the Haynes SP-16 specimen, while no cracking can be observed in the ORNL 30-38 alloy.
As was expected, the Inconel specimen cracked extensively, because no columbium-modified filler metal (known as INCO 62) was added.
Another type of crack test, designated as the open-end slot test, utilizes a $2\frac{1}{2} \times 5$ -in. specimen of $\frac{1}{2}$ -in. plate.
A 3-in.-long, $\frac{1}{16}$ -in.-wide slot is cut from one end, and a fusion pass is made from the open end of the slot toward the closed end.

Quenching experiments by Roy, Roy, and Osborn established a value of $545^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ , which was confirmed by Eichelberger et al. of Mound Laboratory.

Recently, however, Kirkina, Novoselova, and Simanov, who studied the system $\mathsf{BaF}_2$ - $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ by thermal analysis and x-ray diffraction, stated that $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ crystallizes in a new modification that is stable above $545^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ when $\mathsf{BaF}_2$ is present.

They claim that $545^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ is a transition temperature between the two forms of $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ and cite the existence of a eutectic at $600^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ between $\mathsf{BaBeF}_4$ and $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ as proof.
Recent quenches of mixtures containing $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ and up to 5 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{BaF}_2$ have not confirmed the Russian work.
When the samples were equilibrated at and quenched from temperatures above $550^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ , only isotropic material and quench growth were

The ordinary variety of $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ was found by petrographic examination of quenches from below $550^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ , and x-ray diffraction revealed ordinary $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ along with the compound found at higher temperatures.

Since this material coexists in equilibrium with $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ below $550^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ , it is, presumably, $\mathsf{BaBeF}_4$ and it cannot be a high-temperature modification of $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ .
These experiments present additional confirmation of the $545^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ melting temperature of $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ .
2.18 for the simple system $\mathsf{LiF - BeF}_2$ represents data from Roy, Roy, and Osborn; Novoselova, Simanov, and Yarembash; and the Oak Ridge National Laboratory.

The low cross sections and generally good nuclear properties of $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ and $\mathsf{Li}^7\mathsf{F}$ , along with the very low liquidus temperatures available in mixtures containing from 35 to 70 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ , make this system of obvious interest as a fuel solvent or as a coolant.

Mixtures that contain between 40 and 65 mole % BeF $_2$ may be of interest to the program.

Only single-phase material was found just below $287^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ , but the solid-phase reaction in which NaF·LiF·3BeF $_2$ is formed proceeds very slowly at only a few degrees lower.
The primary phase field of the ternary compound is small and lies near the composition 50 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{BeF}_2 - 25$ mole $\%$ LiF.

Although exact determinations of ternary eutectics and peritectics have not yet been completed, it is apparent that a region with melting points below $300^{\circ}C$ exists in the neighborhood of the primary phase field of NaF.LiF.3BeF.2.
Compositions in this region (within 5 to 10 mole $\%$ of 50 mole $\%$ BeF-25 mole $\%$ LiF) may be of interest as lowmelting reactor coolants or fuel carriers.

The binary system $\mathsf{BeF}_2\cdot \mathsf{UF}_4$ shows no intermediate compounds and has a eutectic at a concentration of about 1 mole $\%$ UF $^4$ and a temperature of $530^{\circ}C.$ The ternary systems LiF-BeF $^2$ -UF $^4$ and NaF-BeF $^2$ -UF $^4,$ which have been examined in detail at the Mound Laboratory, are shown in Figs.
2.23 is very similar to the $\mathsf{BeF}_2\cdot \mathsf{UF}_4$ system.
A single eutectic, that melts at $530^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ , was found which contains 1.5 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ThF}_4$ .

2.24, shows the LiF-ThF system to have somewhat higher liquidus temperatures than those of its uraniumcontaining counterpart.
LiF-ThF $^4$ compositions melt below $1100^{\circ}F,$ however, and such mixtures would seem, in principle, to be possible breeder blanket materials.

2.25) shows that $4\mathrm{NaF}\cdot \mathrm{ThF}_4$ decomposes at $568^{\circ}C.$ It appears that the Russian workers have interpreted these thermal effects as a transition between two forms of $2\mathrm{NaF}\cdot \mathrm{ThF}_{4'}$ which were suggested by Zachariasen; one of these two forms of $2\mathrm{NaF}\cdot \mathrm{ThF}_{4'}$ which may be prepared by Zachariasen's technique, has been shown in this Laboratory to be metastable.

The $\mathsf{LiF - BeF}_2\text{-ThF}_4$ and the NaF-BeF-ThF 4 systems are remarkably similar to their UF 4 containing analogs.

In the LiF-BeF-UF system, for example, two ternary eutectics occur that melt at 350 and $435^{\circ}C$ and contain 1 mole $\%$ UF-52 mole $\%$ BeF and 8 mole $\%$ UF-26 mole $\%$ BeF, respectively, and the ternary eutectics for the ThF- containing system are nearly identical in composition and temperature.

Previous studies indicated that the solubility of $\mathsf{UF}_3$ in $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ -containing melts was less than 2 wt % at $600^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ , and similar low solubilities were anticipated for $\mathsf{PuF}_3$ .
The first solubility determinations were made with the 57 mole $\%$ NaF-43 mole $\%$ BeF $_2$ mixture, which is reported to melt at $360^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (ref 10).
Nickel filtration apparatus similar to that developed for obtaining UF $_3$ solubility data was used, but it was slightly modified to permit use of smaller amounts (6 g) of fused salts.

Mixtures prepared by holding weighed portions of purified solvent and PuF $_3$ in a mixed atmosphere of argon, hydrogen, and HF for about 1 hr at the desired temperature were filtered by application of vacuum and inert-gas pressure.
The nickel filter, which was welded into the end of a long piece of $\frac{3}{8}$ -in. nickel tubing, was kept out of the melt prior to the actual filtration operation.
The data obtained in the first experiments are shown in Table 2.5.

Several of the mixtures were also examined spectrophotometrically for the presence of $\mathsf{Pu}^{4+}$ in dissolved portions of the fused salt, and nearly all samples have been examined with a petrographic microscope mounted in a glove box adjacent to the stainless steel processing box.

The system must, however, at ultimate equilibrium, satisfy $K_{a}$ at all temperatures and locations in the system with the fixed concentrations of $\mathsf{CrF}_{2^{\prime}}$ , $\mathsf{UF}_{3^{\prime}}$ and $\mathsf{UF}_{4^{\prime}}$ .

Inconel systems with high- and low-temperature regions at 1600 and $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in which NaK-KF-LiF-UF $_4$ mixtures are circulated and Hastelloy-X systems operated at similar temperatures with the fuel mixture NaF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ belong to the class of systems in which chromium deposits occur.

Measurements of the rate of loss of radiotracer chromium from an inert fluoride melt exposed to Inconel showed the overall diffusion rate for chromium into Inconel to be $3.7 \times 10^{-15} \, \text{cm}^2/\text{sec}$ at $700^{\circ}\text{C}$ ; the activation energy of the process is 66 kcal/g-at.

Relatively little information is available regarding the effect of temperature on the equilibrium constant in $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ -containing systems, but preliminary values obtained for an LiF-BeF $_2$ (48-52 mole %) solvent are compared in Table 2.6 with those for NaF-ZrF $_4$ solvents for which many corrosion tests have been made.

A systematic study of the solubility of these materials in NaF-ZrF<sub>4</sub> and some other mixtures has been made, and the status of knowledge concerning behavior of these materials is summarized below.

The solubilities of the noble gases in molten NaF-ZrF₄ (53-47 mole %) obey Henry's law, increase with increasing temperature, decrease with increasing atomic radius, and are not appreciably affected by the presence of 10 wt % UF₄.

The heats of solution are, in the same order, 6.2, 8.0, 8.2, and 11.1 kcal/mole.

The increase in solubility with increasing temperature is in marked contrast to the behavior of HF in this solvent; the Henry's law constant for HF at $600^{\circ}C$ is $1.23 \times 10^{-5}$ , while the heat of solution is $-4.5\mathrm{kcal / mole}$ .

The solubility of krypton has not been examined, but there is little reason to doubt that it dissolves in $\mathsf{NaF - ZrF}_4$ (53-47 mole %) at $600^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ to the extent of $3\pm 1\times 10^{-8}$ moles/cm³/atm with a heat of solution of $9.5\pm 1.5\mathrm{kcal / mole}$ .
The rare-earth trifluorides are sparingly soluble in $\mathsf{NaF - ZrF_4}$ (53-47 mole %) mixtures.
In this solvent the solubility of $\mathsf{CeF}_3$ is 2.3 mole % at $600^{\circ}C$ and 4.3 mole % at $800^{\circ}C$ .
The product of the solubility (in mole %) and the third power of the cationic radius is constant to within $\pm 3\%$ for $\mathsf{CeF}_3$ , $\mathsf{LaF}_3$ , and $\mathsf{SmF}_3$ .
The solid phase in equilibrium with a saturated solution of two rare-earth fluorides in the NaF-ZrF $_4$ (53-47 mole %) mixture is a nearly ideal solid solution of the rare-earth fluorides.

If the fuel were NaF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ , considerable CeF $_3$ (perhaps 2 mole %) would be carried in the circulating stream.
Preliminary experiments on the solubility of $\mathsf{CeF}_3$ in an NaF-BeF $_2$ (57-43 mole %) mixture produced the data presented in Table 2.7.
A comparison of the values for the NaF-BeF $_2$ and NaF-ZrF $_4$ mixtures, as read from smooth curves through the experimental points, is given in Table 2.8.

section: METHODS FOR PURIFICATION OF MOLTEN SALTS
text: In this method the blended raw materials are charged into reaction vessels of copper-lined stainless steel that have capacities of 5, 10, or 50 lb.

section: METHODS FOR PURIFICATION OF MOLTEN SALTS
text: The materials are melted and brought to a temperature of $800^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ under a flowing atmosphere of HF to remove water with a minimum of hydrolysis.

section: METHODS FOR PURIFICATION OF MOLTEN SALTS
text: The $800^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ melt is sparged with dry hydrogen to reduce penta- and hexavalent uranium compounds, sulfate, and extraneous oxidants.

section: METHODS FOR PURIFICATION OF MOLTEN SALTS
text: A final 15- to 24-hr sparging with hydrogen serves to remove the $\mathsf{CuF}_2$ , along with any $\mathsf{FeF}_2$ and $\mathsf{NiF}_2$ that was originally present in the melt, by reducing the compound to metal.
